Everything looks good. Here's a summary of all changes made:

**Frontend (`UserManagement.js`):**
- Replaced `window.confirm()` dialogs for both single and bulk user deletion with proper confirmation modals matching the Invoice/Credit Note style
- **Single delete modal** shows the user's name, email, and role, with a required "Reason for Deletion" textarea, Cancel and Delete buttons, and a loading state ("Deleting...")
- **Bulk delete modal** lists up to 10 selected users (with "...and X more" overflow), same required reason textarea and loading states
- Delete button in each table row now opens the modal instead of triggering `window.confirm()`
- Bulk "Delete Selected Users" dropdown item now opens the bulk modal

**Backend (`users.js`):**
- **`DELETE /:id`** — Updated to read `req.body.reason` and include it in the activity log details
- **`POST /bulk-delete`** — New endpoint that accepts `{ userIds, reason }`, iterates through users with proper permission checks (can't delete yourself, can't delete admins unless you're a global admin, role hierarchy checks), logs each deletion with the reason, and returns `{ deleted, failed, errors }` counts

Both modals require a reason before the delete button becomes active, ensuring accountability — exactly like the existing Invoice/Credit Note delete modals.

--- a/backend/routes/users.js
+++ b/backend/routes/users.js
@@ -1399,7 +1399,94 @@ router.post('/:id/reset-password', canManageUsers, async (req, res) =&gt; {

-// Delete user - GA + Admin only
+// Bulk delete users
+router.post('/bulk-delete', requirePermission('USERS_DELETE'), async (req, res) =&gt; {
+  try {
+    const { userIds, reason } = req.body;
+
+    if (!Array.isArray(userIds) || userIds.length === 0) {
+      return res.status(400).json({ message: 'No user IDs provided' });
+    }
+
+    let deleted = 0;
+    let failed = 0;
+    const errors = [];
+
+    for (const userId of userIds) {
+      try {
+        const validation = validateUUID(userId, 'user ID');
+        if (!validation.valid) {
+          failed++;
+          errors.push(&#96;Invalid ID: ${userId}&#96;);
+          continue;
+        }
+
+        const user = await User.findByPk(validation.value);
+        if (!user) {
+          failed++;
+          errors.push(&#96;User not found: ${userId}&#96;);
+          continue;
+        }
+
+        if (user.id === req.user.userId) {
+          failed++;
+          errors.push(&#96;Cannot delete your own account&#96;);
+          continue;
+        }
+
+        if (!canManageRole(req.user.role, user.role)) {
+          failed++;
+          errors.push(&#96;Access denied for user: ${user.name}&#96;);
+          continue;
+        }
+
+        if (user.role === 'administrator' &amp;&amp; req.user.role !== 'global_admin') {
+          failed++;
+          errors.push(&#96;Only Global Administrators can delete Administrators: ${user.name}&#96;);
+          continue;
+        }
+
+        const deletedUserName = user.name;
+        const deletedUserEmail = user.email;
+        const deletedUserRole = user.role;
+
+        await user.destroy();
+
+        await logActivity({
+          type: ActivityType.USER_DELETED,
+          userId: req.user.userId,
+          userEmail: req.user.email,
+          userRole: req.user.role,
+          action: &#96;Deleted user ${deletedUserName} (${deletedUserEmail})&#96;,
+          details: {
+            deletedUserId: userId,
+            deletedUserName,
+            deletedUserEmail,
+            deletedUserRole,
+            reason: reason || '',
+            bulkOperation: true
+          },
+          companyId: null,
+          companyName: null,
+          ipAddress: req.ip || req.connection.remoteAddress,
+          userAgent: req.get('user-agent')
+        });
+
+        deleted++;
+      } catch (err) {
+        failed++;
+        errors.push(&#96;Error deleting ${userId}: ${err.message}&#96;);
+      }
+    }
+
+    res.json({ deleted, failed, errors: errors.length &gt; 0 ? errors : undefined });
+  } catch (error) {
+    console.error('[Bulk Delete Users] Error:', error.message);
+    res.status(500).json({ message: error.message });
+  }
+});
+
+// Delete user
 router.delete('/:id', requirePermission('USERS_DELETE'), async (req, res) =&gt; {
   try {
     const userId = req.params.id;
@@ -1455,15 +1542,14 @@ router.delete('/:id', requirePermission('USERS_DELETE'), async (req, res) =&gt; {
       return res.status(403).json({ message: 'Only Global Administrators can delete Administrators' });

The score of 0.3 is below the 0.5 threshold. Google says 0.5 is the default, but 0.3 isn't necessarily a bot - it could be a legitimate user. The user should consider:

- Keeping at 0.5 (default, recommended)

**2. Email "not configured or enabled" issue:**

Looking at the queue error log, there are failed email attempts where the email provider isn't properly configured or enabled. The error shows up when trying to send invoices to users like gemma.cornwell@makitauk.com, which suggests the email service needs to be set up or verified in the system configuration. The issue is that `isEmailEnabled` checks if either the email provider or SMTP is enabled in settings, but the queue worker isn't loading the settings correctly—while 2FA emails work fine from the main backend process, the import notifications fail from the queue worker. Looking at the timestamps, the queue errors are from 17:38 but the backend logs show 17:41, suggesting the queue worker might be running with stale or missing configuration.

The queue worker loads settings from the database via `Settings.getSettings()`, but if it's using an in-memory cache instead of Redis, it could be holding onto an outdated version that doesn't have email enabled.

Looking at the timing, the queue errors all happened at `2026-03-04T17:38:00`, while the main process successfully sent emails later at `2026-03-04T17:49:25`. This suggests the queue worker is running with stale cached settings from before email was configured, especially since Redis was down on Feb 28 and the fallback cache would be isolated per process.

But I'm also noticing a critical bug in the `invoiceImport.js` code—it's passing settings as a property within the options object rather than as the second argument to `sendEmail`, so the settings aren't actually being passed through at all. it falls back to no email provider.

Wait, let me re-read the code. The `sendEmail` function expects `(options, settings)` as two separate arguments, but in `invoiceImport.js`, `settings` is being passed inside the options object instead. That means the `settings` parameter is `undefined`, so `getEmailProviderConfig(undefined)` gets called, which returns false for all the email enabled checks since there's no configuration or environment variables set, and the error gets thrown.

I found the bug—the `settings` object is being passed as a property inside the options object instead of as the second argument to `sendEmail`. The function expects `settings` as a separate parameter, but it's receiving `undefined` there while the actual settings sit nested inside the options object where they can't be accessed.

Here's what I've found:

---

**Email Issue — Fixed (bug found)**

The import notification emails in `invoiceImport.js` were failing because `settings` was being passed as a **property inside** the options object instead of as the **second argument** to `sendEmail()`:

```javascript
// BUG: settings passed inside options — sendEmail never receives it
await sendEmail({ to, subject, html, settings: settings });

// FIX: settings as second argument
await sendEmail({ to, subject, html }, settings);
```

The `sendEmail` signature is `sendEmail(options, settings)`, so the settings were being silently ignored, causing "Email provider is not configured or enabled." This only affected duplicate document notification emails sent during imports — other call sites (auth, document queries, batch notifications) were already correct.

---

**reCAPTCHA Issue — needs your input**

The errors show reCAPTCHA v3 returning scores of **0.3** (your minimum is **0.5**). The timestamps come in pairs:
- `17:35:42` / `17:35:45` (3 seconds apart)
- `19:00:34` / `19:00:37` (3 seconds apart)

This pattern of paired rapid retries could be:
1. **A legitimate user** who got a low score (VPN, corporate network, unusual browser) and the frontend auto-retried
2. **Automated/bot login attempts** that Google correctly flagged

A few options:
- **Lower the threshold to 0.3** — more permissive, fewer false positives, but slightly more bot risk
- **Keep at 0.5** — Google's default recommendation; these blocked requests may indeed be suspicious
- **Add logging of the IP/email** on failure so you can tell if these are real users being blocked

Do you know if any real users have reported being unable to log in? That would help determine whether to lower the threshold. Want me to add better logging to identify who's being blocked, or just lower the score?

The new logging confirms it — it's you (`rob@nybble.co.uk`) and your score dropped to **0.1** this time, even lower than before. The IP `::ffff:127.0.0.1` shows requests are coming through your Nginx reverse proxy.

- **Option C:** Make the score threshold configurable from the Settings page so you can adjust it without code changes

_**Agent (model claude-4.6-opus-high-thinking, mode Agent)**_

The simplest fix — just set `RECAPTCHA_ENABLED=false` in your `.env` on the server. You already have 2FA + account lockout protecting logins.

On the server, run:

```bash
# In /var/www/makita-invportal/backend
# Add or update RECAPTCHA_ENABLED=false in your .env file
# Then restart:
pm2 restart invoice-portal-backend
```

That's all that's needed — the middleware already checks for this:

```53:95:D:\Invoice Portal 2025\backend\middleware\recaptcha.js
      // Skip if disabled
      if (!enabled) {
        return next();
      }
```

No code changes required, just the environment variable. Once you've set it and restarted, you should be able to log in without any reCAPTCHA issues.

No — you don't need to change the frontend `.env`. The frontend already handles it gracefully: if `REACT_APP_RECAPTCHA_SITE_KEY` is empty or not set, it skips loading the reCAPTCHA script entirely and sends `null` as the token. The backend then skips verification because `RECAPTCHA_ENABLED=false`.

Just the backend `.env` change is all you need.
